planting.note: Sow seeds in spring after soaking in warm water for 24–48 hours or nicking the seed coat to break dormancy; germination is most reliable at soil temperatures above 75°F. Transplant seedlings to their permanent site while still small, as the taproot resents disturbance.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; Golden Shower Tree tolerates a brief dry season and actually flowers most spectacularly when given a short period of drought stress before spring rains or irrigation resumes. The pendulous clusters of bright yellow flowers emerge before or alongside the new foliage, creating a spectacular canopy display that can last several weeks. Allow 15–30 feet of space in all directions, as the tree develops a broad, rounded crown over time. The long, dark seed pods that follow are ornamental in their own right but should be collected and disposed of to prevent unwanted seedlings.

common_name: Pink and White Shower Tree / Java Cassia

planting.note: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or nick with a file before sowing to improve germination rates; sow in spring when temperatures are consistently warm. Container-grown nursery stock establishes more predictably than direct seeding and blooms sooner.

directions: Plant in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and ample room for the tree's characteristically wide, horizontal branching structure; avoid sites with standing water or heavy clay. Java Cassia blooms most heavily when dry-season stress triggers flowering, so reduce irrigation in late winter before the expected spring bloom flush. The bicolor pink-and-white flowers open in waves across the canopy and are highly attractive to bees and butterflies. Stake young trees for the first one to two seasons to develop a strong central leader before the spreading canopy takes over.

planting.note: Scarify seeds lightly and sow directly in spring; germination is fast in warm soil. In zone 8, start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant out after danger of frost passes.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained to dry soil; Buttercup Bush is drought-tolerant once established and thrives on neglect in sandy or rocky soils where other shrubs struggle. The cheerful golden-yellow flowers bloom for an extended season from summer into fall, providing reliable late-season forage for native bees and other pollinators. In zone 8, hard freezes will kill the top growth to the ground, but the plant reliably resprouts from its root system in spring. Cut frosted stems back to the crown in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth and a tidy shrub form.

planting.note: Do NOT plant in Florida where this species is a listed invasive; in other zone 9–11 regions, sow fine seeds on the surface of moist soil without covering, as they require light to germinate. Nursery-grown transplants establish more reliably than seed.

directions: Outside of Florida, plant Paperbark in full sun in moist to wet soils where its tolerance of flooding, salt spray, and periodic drought makes it an exceptional landscape tree for difficult sites. The distinctive spongy, white papery bark peels in thick layers year-round and is one of the most visually striking bark textures available in subtropical horticulture. Creamy white bottlebrush-like flower spikes appear multiple times per year and attract nectar-feeding birds and insects. This species is federally listed as invasive in Florida and must not be planted there; gardeners in other Gulf Coast and Pacific Coast subtropical regions should confirm local regulations before planting.

planting.note: Sow fresh seeds in spring as soon as possible after extracting from ripe fruit; viability drops quickly with dry storage. Plant in deep containers or in situ at final spacing, as the taproot is sensitive to transplant disturbance.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained soil with consistent moisture during the growing season; Soncoya is intolerant of frost and prolonged waterlogging but handles the heat and humidity of tropical climates better than its relative cherimoya. The large, spiny, orange-brown fruits weigh up to several pounds each and develop on the branches and trunk; they are ready to harvest when the skin begins to soften and the segments start to separate. The orange, fibrous interior pulp is most commonly processed into drinks, sorbets, and frozen desserts rather than eaten fresh due to its strong flavor. Hand pollination with a small brush, transferring pollen between flowers on different trees, can significantly improve fruit set.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System
Morton, J. Fruits of Warm Climates
